era_context: The late 13th century continues with the Mongol Empire fragmenting into regional khanates, the Yuan Dynasty consolidating Han Chinese territories, the Mamluk Sultanate dominating the eastern Mediterranean, and European feudal monarchies centralizing power. The Crusader era is ending; the age of European maritime expansion has not yet begun.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Central Asian steppe (oral traditions and fragmentary sources)

events.title: Battle of Falkirk (Second Battle of Falkirk) / Edward I defeats Scottish forces
events.description: Edward I of England defeats a Scottish army at Falkirk, a major engagement in the First Scottish War of Independence. The English victory demonstrates the effectiveness of English military organization and longbow tactics against Scottish schiltrons (pike formations). However, the battle does not end Scottish resistance; the conflict continues for decades. The battle is significant for military history as an early demonstration of the longbow's tactical dominance.
key_figures: William Wallace (Scottish commander, though not present at this battle)

sources.name: English royal chronicles and military records

events.certainty_note: The battle is well-documented in English administrative records and near-contemporary chronicles. The date of July 22, 1298 is firmly established.

events.title: Venetian-Genoese naval conflict / Battle of Curzola
events.description: A major naval battle between Venetian and Genoese fleets near the island of Curzola (Korčula). The Genoese fleet, commanded by Lamba Doria, defeats the Venetian fleet under Andrea Dandolo. The battle is a significant moment in the long Venetian-Genoese rivalry for Mediterranean dominance. According to tradition, the Venetian merchant Marco Polo is captured in this battle, though this claim is disputed by modern scholarship.
key_figures: Andrea Dandolo (Venetian admiral, killed in battle)
Marco Polo (Venetian merchant, allegedly captured; disputed)

sources.name: Venetian and Genoese chronicles (Marino Sanudo, Genoese sources)
Marco Polo's account in 'Il Milione' (The Travels of Marco Polo)

events.certainty_note: The battle itself is well-documented in Venetian and Genoese sources. However, Marco Polo's presence and capture are disputed; some scholars argue he was already in Venice by this date or that the account conflates multiple events.

events.title: Yuan Dynasty administrative reforms under Temür Khan's faction
events.description: Internal factional struggles within the Yuan Dynasty continue as different Mongol princes and Han Chinese officials compete for influence. By 1298, the Yuan Dynasty maintains control over China but faces growing administrative challenges and Han Chinese resistance. The dynasty's reliance on Mongol military elites and Han Chinese bureaucratic expertise creates ongoing tension.
key_figures: Temür Khan (Mongol prince, later Emperor Wuzong)

sources.name: Yuanshi (Official History of the Yuan Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: Yuan Dynasty administrative records and official histories document factional politics and court dynamics.

events.title: Mamluk military campaigns and consolidation in the Levant
events.description: The Mamluk Sultanate under al-Nasir Muhammad continues military consolidation in the Levant and maintains control of trade routes. The sultanate faces threats from Mongol incursions from the north and manages internal factional conflicts among Mamluk emirs. The period is marked by relative stability compared to earlier decades, allowing for economic recovery and architectural patronage.
events.certainty_note: Mamluk administrative records and near-contemporary chronicles provide detailed documentation.

events.title: English Parliament and feudal politics under Edward I
events.description: Edward I of England continues to consolidate royal power and develop parliamentary institutions. By 1298, Edward has summoned multiple parliaments to raise funds for his Scottish and French campaigns. These parliaments, while not yet democratic, establish precedents for consultation with nobles and commons that shape English political development.

sources.name: English royal records and parliamentary rolls

events.certainty_note: English royal administrative records and parliamentary rolls provide detailed documentation of political institutions.

disconfirming_evidence: The claim that Marco Polo was captured at the Battle of Curzola in 1298 is disputed by modern scholarship. Some historians argue that Polo was already in Venice by this date or that the account in 'Il Milione' conflates multiple events or is a later interpolation. The exact circumstances of Polo's return to Venice remain uncertain. Additionally, the significance of the Battle of Curzola in determining Mediterranean dominance may be overstated; Venetian naval power recovered relatively quickly, and the battle did not decisively shift the balance of power between the two republics.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1298 is moderate and regionally uneven. European sources (English chronicles, Venetian and Genoese records) are relatively abundant. East Asian sources (Yuan Dynasty records) are well-preserved. Islamic sources (Mamluk records) are detailed. However, the surviving record is heavily weighted toward elite political and military events; economic, social, and cultural history are underrepresented. Sub-Saharan African, Southeast Asian, and Pacific sources are minimal or absent. The sources reflect the biases of literate, centralized states; oral traditions and non-elite perspectives are largely lost. The Battle of Curzola narrative, in particular, has been shaped by later Venetian and Genoese historiography, which may emphasize dramatic military encounters over longer-term economic and political trends.

graph_edges.note: Battle of Falkirk is part of the ongoing First Scottish War of Independence
Battle of Curzola is part of the broader Venetian-Genoese rivalry
